checks.implication: The weak BitNet-SFT result is unlikely to be caused only by task formatting or dataset split.
The short/default run is undertrained and should not be used as the final BitNet-SFT anchor.

checks.check: Best completed budget BitNet-SFT matches paper anchor
checks.implication: The local blocker has shifted from BitNet-SFT viability to BitDistill/FP16-level recovery.

checks.check: Best completed budget BitNet-SFT is still below paired FP16
checks.implication: Clearing the paper BitNet-SFT anchor is not enough; the BitDistill stage must recover the remaining FP16 gap.

checks.check: BitNet-SFT ternary projection count matches Qwen2.5-0.5B decoder projections
checks.implication: The low baseline is not explained by exporting only one ternary tensor or missing whole decoder projection families.
The poor score is not caused by accidentally ternarizing the classification head in this checkpoint.

checks.check: SubLN-only BitNet-SFT control explains the gap
checks.implication: Current SubLN insertion alone does not recover the paper anchor; either the SubLN recipe differs or it requires matched warmup/search.
This separates weight-code collapse from activation-quantization damage.
The next controlled comparison should use the best budget row, not the default 1000-step row.

checks.evidence: TernaryWeightSTE implements alpha=mean(abs(W)), round(W/alpha), clamp [-1,1], with identity STE backward.
checks.implication: The tensor-scale path matches the paper-style absmean ternary projection. Row-scale remains a fork variant.

checks.evidence: AbsmaxActivationSTE uses per-token absmax / 127 with int8 clamp and identity backward.
checks.implication: A8 is present and can be ablated with --no-activation-quantization.
Sequence-classification head treatment is not the obvious reason for the weak early BitNet-SFT row.
Placement is mechanically consistent with the paper description, but the exact timing/init still matters.
All Q/K/V/O and MLP gate/up/down decoder projections are represented as ternary tensors.
The audit does not find accidental ternarization of embeddings, norms, or the sequence-classification head.
The audited paper-style checkpoint stores one scalar scale per projection tensor, not row-scale metadata.
Storage has the expected 1.58-bit ternary alphabet; this is also the information bottleneck that blind PTQ cannot avoid.
checks.item: H. Ternary code distribution is three-symbol

checks.implication: Disabling activation quantization changes MNLI only slightly in the local control; ternary training/recipe dominates the gap.
checks.item: A/B. A8 ablation is not the primary gap

checks.implication: SubLN should be treated as architecture surgery requiring matched warmup/distillation, not as a harmless drop-in for short SFT.
checks.item: I/J. SubLN is not identity-preserving locally

checks.implication: The earliest BitNet-SFT failure was substantially undertraining/schedule. The active blocker is now FP16-level BitDistill recovery.
checks.item: Budget explanation for weak early baseline

interpretation: The audited implementation clears the basic mechanical checks for the paper-style tensor-scale BitNet-SFT baseline: exact absmean STE equation, A8 path, dense classifier head, 168 decoder projection tensors, scalar tensor scales, and no accidental embedding/norm/head ternarization. The weak early BitNet-SFT row is best explained by budget/schedule, while the remaining research blocker is BitDistill recovery toward FP16 accuracy and the non-identity behavior of SubLN without matched warmup.

subln_activation.interpretation: Local SubLN insertion is not an identity-preserving conversion. It normalizes the tensors entering o_proj/down_proj to unit RMS and can materially perturb logits before any continued pretraining. Therefore a short SubLN-only downstream run is not a decisive test of the paper's Stage-1 recipe.
